title: Tapestry slit weave technique
subtitle: Creating geometric colour blocks with open slits at the boundaries
excerpt: Slit weave is the simplest way to create distinct colour blocks in tapestry weaving. Each colour area is woven independently and the boundaries between colours are open slits in the cloth. This entry covers when to use slits, how to manage their width, and how to close them selectively for structural reasons.
sourceNotes: Slit weave is documented in Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in the Coptic and Peruvian textile scholarship published by the V&A Museum. The technique is among the oldest recorded in tapestry weaving history.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tapestry technique in which adjacent colour areas are woven independently, each turning back on their own warp threads at the colour boundary. This leaves a vertical gap (slit) in the cloth at each boundary.
The vertical threads tensioned on the loom before weaving. In slit weave, each colour works over its own section of warp threads without linking to the adjacent colour.
The horizontal yarn woven through the warp. In slit weave, each colour area uses its own separate length of weft that never crosses into the adjacent area.
An alternative to slit weave in which the two adjacent weft colours link around a shared warp thread at the boundary. Interlocking closes the slit but creates a visible ridge on the surface.

content.text: Slit weave produces the clean, geometric colour blocks seen in Kilim rugs and Coptic textiles. The technique is called slit weave because the boundary between colour areas is a literal opening in the cloth: the two adjacent
colours each work within their own section of
and never cross. The result is a hard, crisp colour boundary with a narrow vertical gap, the
opening, at that line. Short slits (fewer than 8 rows) are structurally stable; longer slits need to be managed.

items.name: Frame loom with a plain-weave warp
Two or more tapestry weft colours on bobbins

content.text: Decide which warp thread marks the colour boundary. Work the colour A weft across its section of the warp and bring it back to the boundary thread. Work the colour B weft across its section in the opposite direction and bring it back to the same boundary thread. Each colour always turns back at this thread without linking to the other.

Continue weaving both areas in parallel, row by row, with both bobbins active. The two colour areas rise at the same rate; if one section gets more rows than the other, the piece will distort. Check that both sides are level every ten rows.
A slit longer than about 15 rows will show through the finished piece and weaken the cloth. Three options will close it: (a) switch to

for a portion of the boundary; (b) sew the slit closed on the back after finishing; (c) use it as a design feature. Kilim rugs use long slits as part of the geometric pattern at corners and star points.

Moving the boundary point one warp thread to the left or right every two rows produces a diagonal edge rather than a vertical slit. A diagonal boundary only two threads wide per two rows gives a 45-degree angle. The slit is eliminated because the boundary never stays at the same thread long enough to open a gap.

items.problem: The colour areas are different heights at the same point in the weave
items.cause: More rows were woven in one area than the other before checking level
items.fix: Work both bobbins one row at a time, alternating: one row colour A, one row colour B. Never complete many rows in one area before working the other.

items.problem: The slit opens to a large gap that distorts the piece
items.cause: The slit was too long before closing or diagonalising
items.fix: Switch to interlocking at the boundary from the point where the slit is already too long, then sew the open section closed with matching thread from the back.
